label: verify-54960-5
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Gov. Code § 54960.5
- § 54960.5

## How the documents used it

[dialectic] …iolation (e.g., by disclosing the full content of the AI-mediated exchanges and conducting a fresh, uncontaminated public deliberation) is void or voidable; and (3) potential fee-shifting to a successful plaintiff under Gov. Code § 54960.5. (Flag for verification: whether serial-meeting violations under § 54952.2(b)(1) are independently curable under § 54960.1's specific listed sections, or must be remedied through general § 54960 declaratory/injunctive r…

[structural] …mics

- The only actor with a clear, immediate financial incentive to litigate is the cure-and-correct petitioner, and that incentive is meaningfully amplified by one-way fee-shifting available to prevailing plaintiffs (Gov. Code § 54960.5 — *verify exact mechanics and whether truly one-way*). This structurally subsidizes a single, possibly non-representative, possibly outcome-motivated private actor to carry what is nominally the "public's" transparency …

[naive_sonnet] …cts* very likely still support a due-process challenge to whatever the council ultimately decides on Harbor Yards. That should drive your recommendation regardless of how the Brown Act question resolves.

Also relevant: § 54960.5 gives a prevailing Brown Act plaintiff attorney's fees, which is a strong incentive for the journalist, a neighborhood group, or the applicant's opponents (or the applicant itself, if it loses) to litigate this rather t…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q1 — The AI assistant as serial-meeting intermediary (Brown Act).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Cal. Gov. Code § 54960.5 (Brown Act; last amended by Stats. 2012, ch. 732)","note":"Section exists and the cite form is correct. It authorizes a court to award costs and reasonable attorney's fees to a plaintiff who prevails in an action under §§ 54960, 54960.1, or 54960.2 by establishing a Brown Act violation, which supports all three snippets' use of it as the plaintiff-side fee-shifting hook. However, the \"structural\" snippet's flagged doubt about it being \"truly one-way\" is well-placed: the same section also lets a prevailing defendant recover fees/costs (in §54960/54960.1 actions) if the plaintiff's suit was \"clearly frivolous and totally lacking in merit\" — so it's asymmetric (favoring plaintiffs, since the plaintiff standard is just prevailing/establishing a violation vs. the defendant's much harder frivolousness bar) rather than a strict one-way fee statute.","source_urls":["https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=GOV&sectionNum=54960.5","https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54960-5/","https://law.justia.com/codes/california/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54960-5/"]}

data.canonical_cite: Cal. Gov. Code § 54960.5 (Brown Act; last amended by Stats. 2012, ch. 732)
data.note: Section exists and the cite form is correct. It authorizes a court to award costs and reasonable attorney's fees to a plaintiff who prevails in an action under §§ 54960, 54960.1, or 54960.2 by establishing a Brown Act violation, which supports all three snippets' use of it as the plaintiff-side fee-shifting hook. However, the "structural" snippet's flagged doubt about it being "truly one-way" is well-placed: the same section also lets a prevailing defendant recover fees/costs (in §54960/54960.1 actions) if the plaintiff's suit was "clearly frivolous and totally lacking in merit" — so it's asymmetric (favoring plaintiffs, since the plaintiff standard is just prevailing/establishing a violation vs. the defendant's much harder frivolousness bar) rather than a strict one-way fee statute.
